does: This paper introduces In-Context Reward Adaptation, a transformer-based framework that dynamically models diverse human preferences without retraining. Rather than relying on static reward models, the approach uses in-context learning to infer underlying reward structures from small sets of preference demonstrations. The key technical contribution involves characterizing why standard transformer architectures exhibit asymptotic bias when modeling rewards, then addressing this limitation by incorporating human response time as an auxiliary input signal. The framework enables language models to adaptively align with unseen preference distributions on-the-fly, demonstrating improved generalization to novel human value domains that were not encountered during training.
connects: This work builds directly on recent advances in Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback, which has become the standard approach for aligning large language models with human preferences. It extends prior multi-reward modeling frameworks that attempt to capture preference heterogeneity across different domains, but recognizes fundamental limitations in fixed-domain approaches. The paper connects to broader research on in-context learning in transformers, leveraging how these models rapidly adapt to new tasks from minimal examples. It also engages with theoretical work on transformer expressivity and bias characterization, while drawing on human behavioral signals like response time—grounding the approach in psychologically-informed signals used in preference learning and cognitive science research on decision-making.
enables: This framework opens several research directions for more adaptive and robust alignment. It suggests pathways toward truly general preference modeling systems that can handle arbitrary human value distributions without model retraining, reducing the cost of customizing language models for diverse user populations. The work enables downstream applications where language models serve heterogeneous users with conflicting preferences, from personalized recommendation systems to context-sensitive policy generation. It points toward richer multi-modal reward modeling by demonstrating how auxiliary behavioral signals improve in-context adaptation, potentially incorporating other metadata like user demographics or interaction history. The theoretical characterization of transformer bias in reward modeling could inform architecture design for other adaptive preference learning tasks.
